questionText: Calculate the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at point P due to the current in the semicircular section of wire shown in Figure 20.66. (Hint: See "Frequently Asked Questions.")
expectedAnswer: B = μ₀I/4R, directed into the page

questionText: A solenoid contains 750 coils of very thin wire evenly wrapped over a length of 15.0 cm. Each coil is 0.800 cm in diameter. If this solenoid carries a current of 7.00 A, what is the magnetic field at its center?

As a new electrical technician, you are designing a large solenoid to produce a uniform 0.150 T magnetic field near its center. You have enough wire for 4000 circular turns, and the solenoid must be 1.40 m long and 2.00 cm in diameter. What current will you need to produce the necessary field?

A solenoid is designed to produce a 0.0279 T magnetic field near its center. It has a radius of 1.40 cm and a length of 40.0 cm, and the wire carries a current of 12.0 A. (a) How many turns must the solenoid have? (b) What total length of wire is required to make this solenoid?

parts.label: a
parts.question: How many turns must the solenoid have?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What total length of wire is required to make this solenoid?

questionText: As shown in Figure 20.67, a single circular current loop 10.0 cm in diameter carries a clockwise 2.00 A current. A long, straight wire is positioned 20.0 cm from the center of the loop and carries a current I. Suppose you wish to use the field of the long wire to cancel out the magnetic field that the loop produces at its center. (a) In what direction should the current in the long wire flow, up or down? (b) What magnitude of current I is needed?

parts.label: a
parts.question: In what direction should the current in the long wire flow, up or down?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What magnitude of current I is needed?

questionText: A solenoid that is 35 cm long and contains 450 circular coils 2.0 cm in diameter carries a 1.75 A current. (a) What is the magnetic field at the center of the solenoid, 1.0 cm from the coils? (b) Suppose we now stretch out the coils to make a very long wire carrying the same current as before. What is the magnetic field 1.0 cm from the wire's center? Is it the same as you found in part (a)? Why or why not?

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the magnetic field at the center of the solenoid, 1.0 cm from the coils?

parts.label: b
parts.question: Suppose we now stretch out the coils to make a very long wire carrying the same current as before. What is the magnetic field 1.0 cm from the wire's center? Is it the same as you found in part (a)? Why or why not?

questionText: You have 25 m of wire, which you want to use to construct a 44-cm-diameter coil whose magnetic field at its center will exactly cancel the earth's field of 0.55 gauss. What current will your coil require?

A toroidal solenoid (see Figure 20.42) has inner radius $r_1 = 15.0\mathrm{cm}$ and outer radius $r_2 = 18.0\mathrm{cm}$. The solenoid has 250 turns and carries a current of 8.50 A. What is the magnitude of the magnetic field at the following distances from the center of the torus: (a) 12.0 cm; (b) 16.0 cm; (c) 20.0 cm?

Three long, straight electrical cables, running north and south, are tightly enclosed in an insulating sheath. One of the cables carries a 23.0 A current southward; the other two carry currents of 17.5 A and 11.3 A northward. Use Ampère's law to calculate the magnitude of the magnetic field at a distance of 10.0 m from the cables.

A long, straight, cylindrical wire of radius R carries a current uniformly distributed over its cross section. At what location is the magnetic field produced by this current equal to half of its largest value? Use Ampère's law and consider points inside and outside the wire.

Platinum is a paramagnetic metal having a relative permeability of 1.00026. If a thin rod of platinum is placed in an external magnetic field of 1.3500 T, with its axis parallel to that field, what will be the magnetic field inside the rod?
When a certain paramagnetic material is placed in an external magnetic field of 1.5000 T, the field inside the material is measured to be 1.5023 T. Find the relative permeability of this material.

A 150 g ball containing 4.00 × 10⁸ excess electrons is dropped into a 125 m vertical shaft. At the bottom of the shaft, the ball suddenly enters a uniform horizontal 0.250 T magnetic field directed from east to west. If air resistance is negligibly small, find the magnitude and direction of the force that this magnetic field exerts on the ball just as it enters the field.

Magnetic balance. The circuit shown in Figure 20.68 is used to make a magnetic balance to weigh objects. The mass m to be measured is hung from the center of the bar, which is in a uniform magnetic field of 1.50 T directed into the pla
expectedAnswer: a) point a is positive  b) 3.21 kg

questionText: A thin $50.0\,\mathrm{cm}$ long metal bar with mass $750\,\mathrm{g}$ rests on, but is not attached to, two metal supports in a uniform $0.450\,\mathrm{T}$ magnetic field, as shown in Figure 20.69. A battery and a $25.0\,\Omega$ resistor in series are connected to the supports. What is the largest terminal voltage the battery can have without breaking the circuit at the supports?

A long, straight wire containing a semicircular region of radius $0.95\,\mathrm{m}$ is placed in a uniform magnetic field of magnitude 2.20 T as shown in Figure 20.70. The wire carries a current of $4.0\,\mathrm{A}$. (a) What is the net magnetic force acting on the two straight sections of the wire? (b) What magnetic field does the wire produce at the center of the hemisphere? (Hint: See "Frequently Asked Questions.")
expectedAnswer: a) 9.7 N, to the left  b) 1.3 × 10⁻⁶ T, into the page

parts.label: a
parts.question: What is the net magnetic force acting on the two straight sections of the wire?

parts.label: b
parts.question: What magnetic field does the wire produce at the center of the hemisphere? (Hint: See "Frequently Asked Questions.")

questionText: A singly charged ion of ${}^{7}\mathrm{Li}$ (an isotope of lithium containing three protons and four neutrons) has a mass of $1.16 \times 10^{-26}\,\mathrm{kg}$. It is accelerated through a potential difference of $220\,\mathrm{V}$ and then enters a $0.723\,\mathrm{T}$ magnetic field perpendicular to the ion's path. What is the radius of the path of this ion in the magnetic field?

An insulated circular ring of diameter $6.50\,\mathrm{cm}$ carries a 12.0 A current and is tangent to a very long, straight, insulated wire carrying $10.0\,\mathrm{A}$ of current, as shown in Figure 20.71. Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at the center of the ring due to this combination of wires.

The effect of transmission lines. Two hikers are reading a compass under an overhead transmission line that is $5.50\,\mathrm{m}$ above the ground and carries a current of $0.800\,\mathrm{kA}$ in a horizontal direction from north to south. (a) Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at a point on the ground directly under the transmission line. (b) One hiker suggests that they walk $50\,\mathrm{m}$ away from the lines to avoid inaccurate compass readings due to the current. Considering that the earth's magnetic field is on the order of $0.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{T}$, is the current really a problem?
expectedAnswer: a) 2.91 × 10⁻⁵ T, toward the east  b) The field is the same order of magnitude as the earth's field.

parts.label: a
parts.question: Find the magnitude and direction of the magnetic field at a point on the ground directly under the transmission line.

parts.label: b
parts.question: One hiker suggests that they walk $50\,\mathrm{m}$ away from the lines to avoid inaccurate compass readings due to the current. Considering that the earth's magnetic field is on the order of $0.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{T}$, is the current really a problem?

questionText:
DATA A current-carrying wire of length $0.15\,\mathrm{m}$ is in a perpendicular magnetic field. The magnetic force on the wire is measured as a function of the current through the wire. The resulting data are given in the table.

|  Current (A) | Force (N)  |
| --- | --- |
|  0.5 | 3.0 × 10⁻³  |
|  0.8 | 9.6 × 10⁻³  |
|  1.4 | 1.7 × 10⁻²  |
|  1.9 | 2.3 × 10⁻²  |

Make a plot of the force as a function of the current. Using a "best fit" to the data, determine the magnitude of the magnetic field.
Two very long, straight wires carry currents as shown in Figure 20.72. For each case shown, find all locations where the net magnetic field due to these wires is zero.
expectedAnswer: a) Equidistant from the two wires  b) 3 times closer to the wire with the smaller current  c) Equidistant from both wires

questionText: A $3.00\,\mathrm{N}$ metal bar, $1.50\,\mathrm{m}$ long and having a resistance of $10.0\,\Omega$, rests horizontally on conducting wires connecting it to the circuit shown in Figure 20.73. The bar is in a uniform, horizontal, $1.60\,\mathrm{T}$ magnetic field and is not attached to the wires in the circuit. What is the acceleration of the bar just after the switch is closed?

A pair of long, rigid, metal rods, each of length $L$, lie parallel to each other on a perfectly smooth table. Their ends are connected by identical, very light, conducting springs of force constant $k$ (Figure 20.74) and negligible unstretched length. If a current $I$ runs through this circuit, the springs will stretch. At what separation will the rods remain at rest? Assume that $k$ is large enough so that the separation of the rods will be much less than $L$.

A plastic circular loop has radius $R$, and a positive charge $q$ is distributed uniformly around the circumference of the loop. The loop is now rotated around its central axis, perpendicular to the plane of the loop, with angular speed $\omega$. If the loop is in a region where there is a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ directed parallel to the plane of the loop, calculate the magnitude of the magnetic torque on the loop.

A long wire carrying $6.50\,\mathrm{A}$ of current makes two bends, as shown in Figure 20.75. The bent part of the wire passes through a uniform $0.280\,\mathrm{T}$ magnetic field directed as shown in the figure and confined to a limited region of space. Find the magnitude and direction of the force that the magnetic field exerts on the wire.
expectedAnswer: 1.97 N, at 68.3° clockwise from the straight left-hand segment
